content: Many developers treating crates like building blocks for a house accidentally try to build the foundation first, but in Rust with egui and eframe, the foundation is actually the rendering loop itself. When you start writing an immediate mode UI library or a crate that wraps egui, your most common mistake isn't forgetting to call draw(); it's misunderstanding how the state machine works between frames. Imagine we are building a simple widget crate called my_ui for our desktop application; instead of thinking about what pixels to place on screen immediately, you must think about what data structures need to be passed through the eframe context so that the next frame can render them correctly. The core concept here is that immediate mode means you describe what should appear in a logical representation rather than describing pixel coordinates directly, and this distinction becomes critical when moving from native Rust apps targeting x86_64 to web targets using wasm. When you compile your code for wasm target, the eframe crate automatically handles the threading complexity by wrapping your main function in an event loop that queues frames, but beginners often write their custom painting logic assuming they can mutate shared state directly across threads like they would in a single-threaded native context. You need to understand that egui provides a specific trait called DrawContext which acts as a bridge between your Rust types and the backend system; this is where you define how your custom widgets interact with eframe's internal buffer management. If you are building a crate that exposes these capabilities, remember that every time you call a method on the ecore loop like update() or poll(), you are essentially telling the runtime to process pending events and potentially render a new frame without blocking the main thread unnecessarily. The analogy is like a restaurant kitchen where your Rust code is the chef's ticket system; if you forget to clear your tray between orders (a common mistake when managing Vec buffers in egui), you will end up with duplicate tickets or lost data just as you would lose customers at the counter. When integrating custom painting, think of each frame as a single transaction where you pass ownership of your temporary state into the DrawContext trait implementation rather than trying to hold onto mutable references that span multiple frames; this is why eframe uses interior mutability patterns like RefCell or Arc<Mutex<T>> internally when needed but expects your high-level API to be thread-safe by default. Finally, remember that while egui's immediate mode feels instant because you describe the layout once and it resolves automatically at render time, the underlying reality requires careful management of frame counts and event queues just like any other Rust system library; if you treat your crate as a simple function returning UI elements without considering how eframe batches these into frames, you will encounter performance issues that mimic a race condition even though no explicit threading is involved. By treating the crate as an orchestrator rather than a direct painter, and by respecting the wasm target's single-threaded event loop model while still leveraging Rust's ownership semantics, you can build robust GUI applications that scale from native platforms to browsers without breaking your core logic.
